nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 3-30 m; juveniles tend to shelter among staghorn corals in inner reef areas.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on filamentous and macroalgae in the wild. In captivity, dried marine algae (nori) offered on a clip several times per week is generally advised, supplemented with spirulina-enriched flake or pellet, and occasional meaty fare such as mysis. A consistent vegetable diet helps reduce HLLE risk.

avoidWith: conspecifics (unless in a very large system)

imageCandidates.notes: H. Zell; 4752x3168. Tack-sharp, vibrant adult with tall sail-like dorsal and anal fins fully erect; clearly shows yellow vertical body striping, white-spotted face, and spotted tail. Most striking sailfin display shot.
Stan Shebs; 1600x1002. Crisp full-profile view, warm vibrant colour showing yellow facial freckles, yellow vertical striping, and blue/white-spotted caudal fin.
Darren Obbard; 2048x1536. Sharp wild in-situ Red Sea shot; whole fish in frame with clear yellow striping, yellow spots, and blue-spotted tail. Adds natural-habitat variety.

summary: The Desjardini Sailfin Tang is a large surgeonfish from the Indian Ocean and Red Sea, recognizable by its tall sail-like fins, yellow facial freckles, and spotted blue tail. Generally considered reef-safe and an active herbivore, it needs a spacious tank of at least 180 cm and tends to be aggressive toward other similarly shaped tangs.
careNotes: The Desjardini Sailfin Tang is the Indian Ocean and Red Sea counterpart to the Pacific Sailfin Tang (Zebrasoma veliferum), and the two species are commonly confused in the trade. Zebrasoma desjardinii is distinguished by yellow facial freckles, vertical body striping, and a blue tail with white spots. Adults reach around 25-40 cm and are active, near-constant swimmers, so tank footprint — particularly length — matters more than depth. Tanks of at least 180 cm in length and around 700 L are generally advised, with some keepers recommending larger for full-grown adults. The Desjardini Sailfin Tang is best kept singly, as it tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other similarly shaped tangs; it is generally peaceful with unrelated reef fish and is widely considered reef-safe. Like other tangs, it appears prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition in which pitting or discoloration develops along the head and lateral line, typically linked to poor water quality or a diet low in marine algae and vitamins. Frequent nori feedings and stable water parameters are commonly recommended to reduce this risk. The caudal peduncle — the narrow base of the tail — bears a sharp, scalpel-like spine that should be respected when netting or handling the fish. Captive lifespan is not well documented for this species specifically, but is generally reported in the range of 8-15 years, in line with other large Zebrasoma tangs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care card: 180 US gal min, semi-aggressive, reef-safe, herbivore, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, max ~16 in.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed 40 cm adult size, family Acanthuridae, herbivore diet, semi-aggressive toward similar species, reef safe. Reports min 500 L; the larger LiveAquaria figure (~680 L / 180 gal) was preferred per the tang-size caution rule and rounded to 700 L.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), max total length 40 cm, native range (Indian Ocean from East Africa and Red Sea east to the Andaman Sea and western Sumatra), depth 3-30 m, environmental temperature 25.4-29.1 C.
